sources.title: The Invisible Man (1933)
sources.description: A mysterious man, whose head is completely covered in bandages, wants a room. The proprietors of the pub aren't used to making their house an inn during the winter months, but the man insists. They soon come to regret their decision. The man quickly runs out of money, and he has a violent temper besides. Worse still, he seems to be some kind of chemist and has filled his room with messy chemicals, test tubes, beakers and the like. When they try to throw him out, they make a ghastly discovery. Meanwhile, Flora Cranley appeals to her father to do something about the mysterious disappearance of Dr. Griffin, his assistant and her sweetheart. Her father's other assistant, the cowardly Dr. Kemp, is no help. He wants her for himself. Little does Flora guess that the wild tales, from newspapers and radio broadcasts, of an invisible homicidal maniac are stories of Dr. Griffin himself, who has discovered the secret of invisibility and gone mad in the process.

sources.title: The Invisible Man (1933)
sources.description: The Invisible Man is a 1933 pre-Code American science fiction horror film directed by James Whale loosely based on H. G. Wells's 1897 novel, The Invisible Man. It  follows Dr. Jack Griffin, a scientist who discovers a potion that makes him invisible but drives him insane. As he wreaks havoc in a small village, his former colleagues and fiancée try to stop him before he descends further into madness and violence.

sources.title: The Invisible Man 1933 colorized (Claude Rains)
sources.description: A scientist (Claude Rains) discovers the secret of invisibility, but in rendering himself invisible, he gradually becomes murderously insane, to the horror of his sweetheart (Gloria Stuart). Claude Rains as Dr. Jack Griffin aka The Invisible Man Gloria Stuart as Flora Cranley William Harrigan as Dr. Arthur Kemp Henry Travers as Dr. Cranley Una O'Connor as Jenny Hall Forrester Harvey as Herbert Hall Holmes Herbert as Chief of Police E.E. Clive as Constable Jaffers I colorized this using AI software that doesn't always get the colors right, but I think it looks fairly natural. Hopefully a more professional job will be done someday.

sources.description: Adaptación de una novela de H.G. Wells sobre un hombre que posee la facultad de hacerse invisible.
